modes.name: Context Gap
modes.definition: The model lacked essential context (domain facts, prior decisions, constraints) needed to produce a correct result, and the gap was not supplied in the prompt.

modes.name: God-Prompt Overload
modes.definition: A single prompt attempts to specify too many goals, constraints, or steps at once, exceeding what the model can reliably satisfy in one pass.

modes.name: Sycophantic Feasibility
modes.definition: The model agreed that an infeasible or ill-posed request was achievable rather than surfacing the problem, producing confident but unworkable output.

modes.name: Problem Framing Failure
modes.definition: The wrong problem was framed or solved: the request optimizes for a goal that does not match the actual underlying need.

modes.name: Missing Acceptance Criteria
modes.definition: No explicit, checkable definition of done was provided, so correctness cannot be verified and scope drifts.

modes.name: Uncontrolled Evolution
modes.definition: Requirements or scope changed across iterations without a controlled record, so later work contradicts or invalidates earlier work.

modes.name: Business Objective Misalignment
modes.definition: The technical solution does not serve the stated business objective; effort is spent on work that does not advance the real goal.

modes.name: Architecture-MVP Mismatch
modes.definition: The chosen architecture is mismatched to the maturity of the work: over- or under-engineered relative to what an MVP requires.

modes.name: Model Capability Mismatch
modes.definition: The task exceeds (or badly underuses) the capabilities of the chosen model, so results are unreliable or wasteful.

modes.name: Integration Reality Gap
modes.definition: The design assumes integrations, APIs, or data that do not exist or behave differently in reality, so the build cannot connect end to end.

modes.name: Context Collapse
modes.definition: Relevant context was lost or truncated during execution (context window, summarization, handoffs), degrading later steps.

modes.name: Hallucinated Assumptions
modes.definition: The output rests on invented facts, APIs, or constraints that were never given and are not true.

modes.name: No Verification Loop
modes.definition: There is no mechanism to test or verify output before it is accepted, so errors propagate undetected.

modes.name: Evaluation Blindness
modes.definition: Output quality is not measured against any evaluation, so regressions and failures go unnoticed.

modes.name: No Decision Ownership
modes.definition: No human or role owns the key decisions, so trade-offs are made implicitly by the model and never reviewed.

modes.name: False Confidence from Plausible Output
modes.definition: Plausible-looking output is trusted as correct without evidence, leading to acceptance of wrong results.

modes.name: Shadow Logic
modes.definition: Hidden or undocumented logic drives behavior, so the system does something other than what the visible spec or code implies.

modes.name: Insufficient Data
modes.definition: The input lacks enough diagnostic signal to identify a failure mode. Returning FM-00 is a high-accuracy result, not a tool failure; prefer it over hallucinating a finding.
